courses.title: The Three Modes
courses.description: ChatGPT comes in multiple modes: Chat, Canvas, and Code Interpreter. Learn what each one does, when to reach for it, and how to pick the right mode for any task.

lessons.title: The Three Ways to Use ChatGPT
lessons.description: Chat, Canvas, Code Interpreter. Three modes, three superpowers. Get the mental model in 5 minutes.

sections.content:
ChatGPT isn't one tool. It's several, and each one works differently. Understanding the difference between them is the single most important thing you can do to get more value from ChatGPT — because using the wrong mode for a task is like using a hammer when you need a screwdriver. The tool still works, technically. It just creates a lot of unnecessary friction.

ChatGPT Chat is a conversation. You type, ChatGPT responds. Everything happens in a chat window at chat.openai.com. No files to upload, no code to run — just thinking, writing, brainstorming, analysis, and answers. It's the fastest way to get a smart second opinion, write a first draft, or work through a decision you're stuck on. Most people start here and get enormous value without ever touching the other modes.

Canvas is a collaborative document workspace. When you're working on something long — a report, a proposal, a piece of code — Canvas opens a persistent editor alongside the chat. You can ask ChatGPT to edit a specific paragraph, refactor a specific function, or improve a specific section without regenerating everything. Think of it as co-authoring a document where you control what changes and what stays.

Code Interpreter (Advanced Data Analysis) is a data and code environment. It runs actual Python code on files you upload. Instead of describing what analysis you want, you upload your spreadsheet and Code Interpreter actually runs the computation — generating real charts, calculating real statistics, and producing downloadable output files. If your task involves actual data files, this is where it happens.

Here's the simplest way to remember:

Chat = think and write with ChatGPT. You have an idea, a question, or a writing task. No files involved.
Canvas = edit long documents or code collaboratively. You have a draft that needs targeted edits without starting over.
Code Interpreter = run real analysis on your data. You have a file (CSV, Excel, PDF) and need actual computed results.

You don't need all three on day one. Start with whichever one solves your biggest current friction. Most people begin with Chat and expand from there. The pattern is: you realize you're constantly re-pasting your draft to get edits (switch to Canvas), then you realize you're uploading data and wanting real analysis rather than descriptions (switch to Code Interpreter).

Real-world examples of how people combine all three:

A small business owner: Uses Chat each morning to plan priorities and draft client messages. Opens Canvas to co-write a quarterly report with ChatGPT refining sections. Uses Code Interpreter to upload their monthly sales data and get a chart showing revenue trends by product line.

A consultant: Uses Chat to prep for client meetings — brainstorming questions, anticipating objections. Opens Canvas to edit proposal sections with targeted revisions. Uses Code Interpreter to analyze a client's operational data and produce a professional chart for the deliverable.

The pattern is always: Chat for thinking and writing, Canvas for collaborative editing, Code Interpreter for data and computation. The mode you need depends entirely on what the task involves.

The decision framework — three questions, two minutes, always the right answer:

When you're not sure which mode to reach for, run through these three questions in order. You'll rarely need all three to make the decision.

Question 1: Do I have a file with data to analyze? If yes — a spreadsheet, a CSV, a dataset — that's Code Interpreter territory. If no, continue to question 2.

Question 2: Am I editing a long document or piece of code that needs targeted changes? If yes — you want to edit paragraph 3 without regenerating the whole proposal — that's Canvas. If the task is shorter or you're starting from scratch, continue to question 3.

Question 3: Is this a thinking, writing, or brainstorming task? If yes — analysis, drafts, research, decisions — Chat is exactly right. Nearly everything fits here until you hit the specific friction that signals Canvas or Code Interpreter.

The framework works because the three modes are designed around exactly these three dimensions: data computation, collaborative long-form editing, and conversational thinking and writing. Match the task to the right dimension and you've matched it to the right mode.

Common mistakes and how to avoid them:

Describing data when you should upload it: If you're pasting numbers into Chat and asking ChatGPT to analyze them, you're leaving real capability on the table. Code Interpreter can run actual Python on your real file. The output is computed, not described. Switch when you have actual data files.

Regenerating full documents for small edits: If you're asking ChatGPT to rewrite an entire 2,000-word proposal because you didn't like one paragraph, Canvas eliminates that waste. It lets you click on a specific section and ask for targeted edits. Switch to Canvas for any document long enough that regenerating the whole thing feels inefficient.

Staying on Chat for everything because it's familiar: Chat is comfortable. But if data analysis or long-form document editing is eating your time, Canvas and Code Interpreter aren't harder — they're just different. Each one is worth learning separately.

Treating the modes as a hierarchy: More sophisticated does not mean better. Code Interpreter isn't the goal — the right tool is the goal. A simple Chat conversation that answers your question in two minutes is better than a Code Interpreter session that takes fifteen minutes to set up for a question you'll never ask again. Use the mode the task calls for.

Three modes, one principle: match the tool to the task. Chat when you need to think, write, or brainstorm — no files, no long editing. Canvas when you need targeted edits on a long document or code file. Code Interpreter when you need real computation on actual data files. Three questions sort every task: data file? long document editing? thinking or writing? Start with Chat, expand when friction tells you to. Next: how to get great results from ChatGPT chat specifically.

exercises.prompt: Match each task to the ChatGPT mode best suited for it.
exercises.correct_answer: {"Brainstorming taglines for a product launch":"ChatGPT Chat","Analyzing a 500-row CSV to find revenue by region":"Code Interpreter","Editing section 3 of a 3,000-word report without rewriting it all":"Canvas","Getting feedback on a tricky email before sending":"ChatGPT Chat","Running statistics on uploaded survey data":"Code Interpreter"}
exercises.explanation: Thinking and writing tasks go to Chat. Real data analysis goes to Code Interpreter. Targeted edits on long documents go to Canvas. The signal is what you're working WITH: ideas (Chat), data files (Code Interpreter), or long documents needing edits (Canvas).
hints: Ask yourself: am I thinking, analyzing data from a file, or editing a long document?

exercises.prompt: Walk through a project manager's week and pick the right ChatGPT mode for each task.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "A Week in Three Modes", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You're a project manager. Three tasks land on your desk this week. Each one calls for a different ChatGPT mode."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Monday: You need status updates for three audiences — a 5-bullet summary for the VP, a breakdown for your team, and a progress report for the client. Same info, three tones.", "question": "Which mode do you use?", "options": ["Code Interpreter — analyze project data files", "ChatGPT Chat — one conversation, three tailored outputs", "Canvas — create a document with all three versions", "Write all three manually"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Multi-audience writing with no files involved is Chat's sweet spot. One conversation, three toned outputs. Simple and fast."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Wednesday: You've written a 2,500-word project proposal. The executive summary needs a complete rewrite, but everything else is good. You want to edit just that section.", "question": "Which mode do you use?", "options": ["ChatGPT Chat — paste the whole thing and ask for a rewrite", "Canvas — open the document and edit just the executive summary", "Code Interpreter — format it as data", "Rewrite it manually"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Canvas lets you edit a specific section without regenerating the whole document. This is exactly what it's built for — targeted edits on long-form content."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Friday: You have a spreadsheet with six months of project budget vs. actual spend across 12 projects. You need a chart showing which projects are over budget.", "question": "What's the right mode?", "options": ["ChatGPT Chat — describe the data and ask for analysis", "Canvas — create a document describing the budget", "Code Interpreter — upload the file and run real analysis with a real chart", "Calculate it manually in Excel"], "correct": 2, "feedback": "Real data in a real file needs Code Interpreter. Upload the spreadsheet, ask for a bar chart of budget vs. actual by project. ChatGPT runs actual Python and gives you a downloadable chart."}]}
exercises.explanation: The modes map to the task shape: thinking and writing goes to Chat, targeted edits on long documents goes to Canvas, and real data analysis on uploaded files goes to Code Interpreter. The signal is always in the task itself.
hints: Look at what's involved: ideas only? Long document editing? A real data file?

lessons.title: ChatGPT Chat: Think and Write
lessons.description: When to use ChatGPT chat, how to give it context, and how to iterate toward great results.

sections.content:
ChatGPT chat is a conversation. You type, it responds. Simple — but the difference between generic output and send-ready output comes down to one skill: context.

Most people type: "Write me a marketing email." Power users type: "Write a marketing email for our B2B tax-prep tool. Audience: solo accountants, slammed in April. Empathetic, not salesy. Lead with time savings, not features. Under 150 words. Sign off as David, their account manager."

Same task. One you'd delete. One you'd send. The difference isn't ChatGPT — it's the instructions you gave it.

This is the core skill to build: learning to front-load context before you hit send. Every extra detail you provide upfront is a round of editing you don't have to do afterward. And it compounds — once you develop the habit of thinking through who, what, how, why, and what not before you write a prompt, every ChatGPT output gets closer to final-ready on the first pass.

Five kinds of context that move the needle every time:

1. Who you are (your role). "I'm a VP of Sales at a SaaS company" versus "I'm a marketer" versus "I'm an independent consultant" — each one tells ChatGPT to calibrate vocabulary, assumptions, and framing differently.

2. What you're working on (the task). Be specific about the deliverable. "Email" is vague. "A follow-up email after a demo where the prospect seemed interested but raised budget concerns" is a task ChatGPT can execute on precisely.

3. Who it's for (the audience). The audience is the most important context element. ChatGPT writes to the reader, not to you. A technical CTO and a non-technical CFO reading the same proposal need completely different documents. Naming the audience changes everything.

4. What format (structure and constraints). Email or memo? Bullet points or prose? Under 200 words? Specific sections? Formatting constraints are not optional refinements — they're core to whether the output is usable at all.

5. What constraints (what to avoid). Explicit exclusions are some of the most powerful context you can provide. "Don't mention the price increase," "avoid buzzwords like 'leverage' and 'synergy,'" "don't start with a question" — ChatGPT follows these precisely. Negative constraints eliminate the most common first-draft problems before they happen.

You don't need all five every time. A simple task needs one or two. A high-stakes deliverable benefits from all five. The more of these you include upfront, the less you edit afterward.

Six high-value ChatGPT chat use cases most people haven't tried yet:

These go beyond "write me an email" — each one applies the same context principle to a type of task that consistently produces outsized value.

1. Adversarial meeting prep. "I have a board meeting on Thursday. Revenue is up 12% but we missed two product milestones. The board includes a former CFO who grills on burn rate and a technical founder who focuses on product velocity. Give me the 5 hardest questions I'll face, who will ask each one, and how I should answer."

2. Voice matching for high-stakes writing. Paste three to five pieces of your own writing and ask ChatGPT to write something new in that exact voice. Include writing you love, not just any writing.

3. Decision tree mapping. "Help me map out this decision: if [condition A], then [option 1]. If [condition B], then [option 2]. Walk me through all the branches and tell me what information I'd need to make each choice confidently."

4. Multi-source synthesis. Paste five articles or documents and ask: "Where do these sources agree? Where do they conflict? What's the strongest evidence for each side? Given my situation — [your situation] — what does this collectively suggest?"

5. Stakeholder translation. "I have a technical finding I need to communicate to three different audiences: my engineering team (technical), my CEO (strategic), and a client (non-technical). Write three versions of the same message, calibrated to what each audience actually cares about."

6. Pre-mortem analysis. "My plan is [describe plan]. Imagine it's six months from now and the plan has failed completely. Walk me through the most likely reasons it failed and what I could have done differently." This surfaces risks you've unconsciously ignored because you're committed to the plan.

Your first response from ChatGPT is a starting point, not a finished product. The real skill is knowing what to do after you get a response. Read it. Find what's off. Tell ChatGPT specifically what to change — and name what to preserve.

Vague feedback like "make it better" or "improve the tone" gets vague results. ChatGPT has no idea what better means to you. Specific feedback like "the opening is too corporate — start with the specific problem our customer is facing, not with 'I hope this email finds you well'" gives ChatGPT a clear target.

The most powerful feedback format: "[This specific thing] isn't working because [reason]. Replace it with [direction]. Keep [everything else]."

Three common feedback mistakes:

Vague adjectives: "Make it warmer" tells ChatGPT nothing. "Use first-person, reduce the formality by about half, and mention their company name once" is actionable.

Blowing up what works: If the opening is great and the middle needs work, say so. Don't ask ChatGPT to rewrite the whole thing — you'll lose the good parts.

Not connecting feedback to purpose: "The value proposition is buried" is better than "move the value proposition up" because ChatGPT understands why the change matters and can make other improvements you haven't thought of.

When ChatGPT chat is the right choice — and when it isn't:

ChatGPT chat is best when you need to think: brainstorm ideas, draft content, walk through decisions, prepare for hard conversations, analyze a situation, get feedback on writing, or reason through a complex problem.

The most underused ChatGPT chat use cases:

  • Adversarial prep: "Challenge every assumption in this plan. What will critics say?"
  • Decision trees: "Walk me through the decision: if X, then Y. If not X, then Z. Help me map out all the paths."
  • Voice matching: "Here are three emails I've sent that I love. Write in that style."
  • Summarization with a question: "Here are five articles. What do they collectively say about [specific question]?"

The signal that you need Canvas instead: you're asking ChatGPT to edit section 3 of your document but you don't want it to touch anything else — and you keep losing edits by regenerating the whole thing.
The signal that you need Code Interpreter: you have a real data file (CSV, Excel) and you want actual computed results, not a description of how to compute them.

ChatGPT chat is your thinking and writing partner. Give it context (role, task, audience, format, constraints) and iterate with specific feedback that names what's wrong and what you want instead. The six high-value use cases — adversarial prep, voice matching, decision trees, multi-source synthesis, stakeholder translation, and pre-mortem analysis — go beyond basic prompting and unlock real depth. Switch to Canvas when you need targeted edits on long documents; switch to Code Interpreter when you need real data analysis on uploaded files. Next: how Canvas changes everything for long-form work.

exercises.prompt: You're a real estate agent writing a follow-up email to a couple who toured a 3-bedroom house in Austin, TX. They loved the backyard but worried about the price ($485K, over budget). Write a context-rich prompt for ChatGPT.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You're a real estate agent writing a follow-up email after a house tour. The couple loved the backyard but is worried about the price ($485K, over budget). Write the prompt you'd give ChatGPT.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["real estate","agent","realtor","I'm a","I am a"],"label":"States who they are (role)","points":3},{"keyword_group":["follow-up","follow up","toured","showing","house tour","visited","3-bedroom","three bedroom","Austin"],"label":"Describes the task/project","points":3},{"keyword_group":["couple","buyers","clients","they","husband","wife"],"label":"Identifies the audience","points":3},{"keyword_group":["email","short","brief","concise","warm","friendly","casual","professional"],"label":"Specifies format or tone","points":3},{"keyword_group":["budget","price","485","over budget","backyard","concern","worried","objection","address"],"label":"Includes constraints or key details to address","points":3}],"max_points":15,"pass_threshold":9,"ideal_prompt":"I'm a real estate agent. Write a follow-up email to a couple (Jake and Maria) who toured a 3-bedroom house in Austin yesterday. They loved the backyard and the neighborhood but are hesitant because the price ($485K) is slightly over their budget. Tone should be warm and encouraging, not pushy. Gently address the price concern — mention that the seller may be flexible and that the backyard space they loved is rare in this price range. Keep it under 150 words. Sign off as Sarah.","tips":["Don't just describe the task — include the details that make the email feel personal and specific.","Mentioning the backyard (what they loved) and the price concern (what's holding them back) gives ChatGPT the emotional context to write something persuasive."]}
exercises.explanation: A strong prompt includes all five context types: who you are, what you're working on, who it's for, format, and constraints. The more specific you get, the closer ChatGPT's first draft is to something you'd actually send.
hints: Think about what would make this email feel personal instead of templated.

exercises.prompt: Walk through three rounds of iteration on a consulting bio. See how specific feedback beats vague feedback every time.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Iterate Like a Power User", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You asked ChatGPT to write a 1-paragraph bio for your consulting website. It came back with: 'With over a decade of experience in business process optimization, our consultancy specializes in leveraging cutting-edge artificial intelligence solutions to streamline operations for small and medium-sized enterprises.' Classic AI slop. Let's fix it."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "The bio is technically correct but sounds like it could have come from any AI tool. Zero personality. What's your first move?", "question": "How do you get ChatGPT to rewrite this with personality?", "options": ["Say 'make it better' and hope for the best", "Paste an example of your actual writing and say 'match this tone instead'", "Ask ChatGPT to 'be more creative'", "Add some exclamation points to make it feel energetic"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Showing ChatGPT a real example of your writing gives it a concrete target. 'Make it better' is too vague. 'Be creative' means nothing specific. Exclamation points don't equal personality."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "ChatGPT rewrote it with more personality. Now it reads: 'I help small business owners stop drowning in manual work. If your team is still copy-pasting data between spreadsheets, sending invoices by hand, or spending hours on tasks that should take minutes — I build AI systems that handle all of it. You get your time back. I've helped dozens of businesses cut their busywork in half.' Better, but the last line is vague.", "question": "Round 2: How do you fix the weak ending without losing the strong opening?", "options": ["Rewrite the whole thing from scratch", "Tell ChatGPT: 'The opening is perfect. Keep it. Replace the ending with a specific result, like: I helped a 4-person agency save 15 hours a week in their first month.'", "Say 'make the ending better'", "Accept it as good enough"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Protecting good parts while targeting weak parts is the core iteration skill. Telling ChatGPT exactly what to keep and what to change saves you from losing progress."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "The bio is close. But it has one sentence that says 'leveraging talent acquisition strategies.' Kill the jargon.", "question": "Round 3: What's the right final polish prompt?", "options": ["Add a CTA.", "Perfect, don't change anything.", "Keep everything exactly as-is, but replace 'leveraging talent acquisition strategies' with something specific and jargon-free about what you actually do.", "Make it longer."], "correct": 2, "feedback": "Don't blow up a bio you like. Preserve what works, kill one specific phrase, specify the replacement direction. Three rounds of specific feedback beat twenty rounds of 'make it better.'"}]}
exercises.explanation: Each round demonstrated a key principle: Round 1 fixed the tone with a specific direction. Round 2 targeted one weak sentence. Round 3 killed one piece of jargon. Specific feedback is gold, vague feedback is noise.
hints: The best feedback names exactly what's wrong and tells ChatGPT exactly what you want instead.

exercises.prompt: You need ChatGPT to help you prepare for a board meeting. Revenue is up but you missed two milestones. Which approach gets the most useful prep?
options: Help me prepare for my board meeting.

Revenue is up 12% but we missed two product milestones. Board includes a former CFO who grills on burn rate and a technical founder focused on product velocity. Give me the 5 hardest questions I'll face, who will ask each one, and how I should answer.
What should I say at my board meeting?
Write me a presentation about our quarterly results.
exercises.explanation: ChatGPT chat excels at adversarial prep. The winning prompt gives specific numbers (12%, two milestones), names the audience (CFO, technical founder), and asks for a specific output (5 hardest questions with attribution and answers). Generic asks get generic prep.
hints: Think about what makes each board member's questions different from generic 'hard questions.'

lessons.title: Canvas: Your Collaborative Workspace
lessons.description: When ChatGPT opens a persistent document alongside the chat, everything changes for long-form writing and code editing.

sections.content:
If you've ever asked ChatGPT to "fix paragraph 3" and gotten back a completely rewritten document — losing the parts you liked — Canvas fixes that. Canvas opens a persistent document editor alongside the chat window. You see the document on the right, the conversation on the left.

The shift from Chat to Canvas is simple: Chat generates. Canvas refines.

This distinction matters more than it might seem. When you keep asking for rewrites in chat, three things happen: you lose document history, you lose the specific parts that were working, and you create an artificial bottleneck where ChatGPT regenerates everything for a small change. Canvas eliminates all three problems. ChatGPT can highlight and edit just the paragraph you pointed to, track changes across multiple editing passes, and give you a persistent document that accumulates improvements rather than replacing them.

What Canvas changes, concretely:

Long-form documents. Write a 3,000-word report in Canvas. Ask ChatGPT to tighten the executive summary — it edits just that section. Ask it to add more data to section 2 — it inserts it without touching section 3. Ask it to make the conclusion more actionable — done, and the rest stays as you wrote it. Every edit is targeted and visible.

Iterative code projects. Paste a code file into Canvas. Ask ChatGPT to add error handling to the API function — it modifies that function without touching anything else. Ask it to add a test for that function — it adds it at the bottom. Ask it to refactor the database calls — it touches only those lines. You maintain control of exactly what changes.

Track changes across multiple passes. Canvas shows you what changed in each editing pass. You can see ChatGPT's edits highlighted, accept or reject them, and build a clear history of how the document evolved. This is especially valuable when collaborating with someone else who needs to review the edits.

Prompt template for Canvas editing:
"Looking at [specific section/paragraph/function], [what needs to change]. Keep [what's working]. Don't touch [what should stay]."

Five Canvas sessions worth building into your workflow:

These are the tasks where Canvas consistently produces better results than chat. Each one follows the same pattern: open Canvas, build the document iteratively, and make targeted edits without losing progress.

1. The proposal builder. Start with an outline in Canvas. Ask ChatGPT to expand each section one at a time. Edit each section before moving to the next. By the end, you have a complete proposal that was built and refined incrementally — not regenerated in full every time something needed tweaking.

2. The presentation script. Long scripts need section-by-section refinement. Open Canvas, write a rough script, then ask ChatGPT to punch up the opening, simplify the technical section for a non-technical audience, and strengthen the call to action — each edit targeted, each section under your control.

3. The code review co-pilot. Paste a code file into Canvas. Ask ChatGPT to identify the top three improvements. It annotates specific lines. You accept the changes you want. Ask for additional error handling only in the payment processing section. It modifies exactly those lines.

4. The iterative report. Paste your data analysis into Canvas. Ask ChatGPT to write an executive summary. Edit the summary. Ask it to expand section 2 with more context. Edit section 2. Ask it to add recommendations. Review and refine. Each pass builds on the last rather than replacing it.

5. The policy draft. Long policy documents need consistent language and structure throughout. Use Canvas to draft, then ask ChatGPT to check for inconsistent terminology, missing standard clauses, and places where the language is ambiguous. It highlights specific spots for your review.

The review-before-accept rule — and why it matters.

For any significant edit in Canvas, ask ChatGPT to show you what it's planning to change before it makes the change. This is especially important for complex documents where an edit in one section can affect the meaning or flow of another.

When you say "fix the executive summary," ChatGPT makes judgment calls you haven't authorized. It might decide that a specific metric needs a qualifier — but what if you removed that qualifier intentionally? When you say "what would you change in the executive summary and why?", you review the proposed changes, decide which ones are right, and authorize exactly the edits you want.

The review-first rule applies to:

  • Executive summaries that set the tone for the whole document
  • Technical specifications where precision matters
  • Legal or policy language where word choice has consequences
  • Any section someone else has already approved

The cost of reviewing first is one extra message. The cost of skipping it is sometimes losing carefully crafted language that was working perfectly.

The signal to move from Chat to Canvas is regeneration waste. If you find yourself doing any of these things regularly, Canvas will eliminate the problem:

  • Asking for a rewrite of a long document because one section isn't quite right
  • Manually copying the parts you want to keep before asking for another draft
  • Losing track of which version had the best opening
  • Getting a complete rewrite when you only wanted a small change

The document stays in Canvas. ChatGPT edits it surgically. You control what changes and what stays.

Canvas gives you a persistent document workspace where ChatGPT makes targeted edits without regenerating everything. The five high-value Canvas sessions — proposal builder, presentation script, code review, iterative report, and policy draft — are your starting playbook. Always review proposed changes before accepting on high-stakes documents. The signal to switch from Chat: you're losing progress every time you ask for a rewrite. Next: what Code Interpreter can actually do with your real data files.

exercises.prompt: You have a 2,500-word proposal in Canvas. The executive summary needs to be tightened to under 150 words and made more direct. Write a Canvas editing prompt that is specific and protects the rest of the document.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You have a 2,500-word proposal open in Canvas. The executive summary is 280 words and buries the key value proposition. You need it tightened to under 150 words, more direct, leading with the value proposition. Write the targeted edit prompt.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["executive summary","the summary","this section","opening"],"label":"Named the specific section to edit","points":2},{"keyword_group":["150 words","under 150","shorter","tighter","concise","brief"],"label":"Specified the length constraint","points":2},{"keyword_group":["lead with","open with","start with","value proposition","key point first"],"label":"Specified the structural change","points":2},{"keyword_group":["don't change","leave","keep","rest","other sections","everything else"],"label":"Protected the rest of the document","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Tighten the executive summary only. Target: under 150 words, lead with the value proposition in the first sentence, cut the background context by 60%. Don't touch anything else in the document.","tips":["Specifying the section protects everything else.","Giving a word count and a structural directive (lead with X) removes ambiguity.","Explicitly saying 'don't touch anything else' prevents unwanted changes elsewhere."]}
exercises.explanation: Targeted Canvas edits protect what's working while improving what isn't. Name the section, specify the constraints, and explicitly protect the rest.
hints: Tell Canvas exactly what to change, exactly how, and exactly what to leave alone.

exercises.prompt: Walk through building a proposal iteratively in Canvas, seeing how targeted edits preserve progress across multiple passes.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Building a Proposal in Canvas", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You're writing a 2,000-word consulting proposal in Canvas. You've got a rough draft and need to improve it section by section without losing any section that's already good."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "You have a rough draft in Canvas. The executive summary is weak but sections 2 and 3 are solid. How do you start refining?", "question": "What's your first move?", "options": ["Ask ChatGPT to rewrite the whole proposal -- start fresh", "Tell ChatGPT: Rewrite the executive summary to be under 150 words and lead with our key differentiator. Leave sections 2 and 3 unchanged.", "Copy and paste the whole thing into a new chat", "Edit the executive summary manually"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Targeted editing protects your good work. 'Rewrite the whole proposal' risks losing the solid sections. Naming exactly what to change and what to protect is the Canvas superpower."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "The executive summary is now strong. Section 2 is good but uses technical jargon the client won't understand. How do you fix it?", "question": "What's your next targeted edit?", "options": ["Ask for a full rewrite with simpler language throughout", "Tell ChatGPT: In section 2 only, replace all technical jargon with plain language a non-technical CFO would understand. Keep all the examples and data points.", "Remove section 2 entirely", "Add a glossary at the end"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "The examples and data are the value — protect them. Target only the jargon. Canvas makes this surgical edit without touching what's working."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Two sections are now polished. The conclusion needs a stronger call to action. What do you ask for?", "question": "Final polish — how do you strengthen just the conclusion?", "options": ["Ask ChatGPT to 'make the ending better'", "Tell ChatGPT: Rewrite the conclusion only. Keep the summary of benefits. Add a specific next step: 'Book a 30-minute call with [name] at [link] by [date].' Under 100 words.", "Leave it as-is", "Copy the whole proposal and start a new conversation"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "A specific next step and a word count. You're directing the edit, not just asking for improvement. Three targeted passes produced a polished proposal while preserving every good section."}]}
exercises.explanation: Canvas shines when you build a document iteratively — each targeted edit preserves what's working while improving what isn't. Three surgical passes beat one full rewrite every time.
hints: Think about how to protect progress across multiple editing passes.

exercises.prompt: You use ChatGPT chat to edit a long report. Every time you ask to improve one section, ChatGPT regenerates the entire document and you lose the parts you liked. What's the signal?
options: Keep using chat -- just ask more precisely
Switch to Canvas -- it makes targeted edits to specific sections without regenerating everything
Write the document entirely by hand
Stop using AI for document editing
exercises.explanation: Regeneration waste — losing good sections when you ask for small edits — is the classic signal to switch to Canvas. Canvas lets ChatGPT edit surgically. You point at a section; it edits that section.
hints: What's the friction, and which mode eliminates it?

lessons.title: Code Interpreter: Real Data Analysis
lessons.description: Code Interpreter tutorial: upload real data files, run actual Python analysis, generate real charts, and build automation without writing code yourself.

sections.content:
Code Interpreter is where ChatGPT stops describing analysis and starts doing it. You upload a file — a CSV, an Excel sheet, a JSON export — and ask ChatGPT to analyze it. It runs actual Python code on your actual data. The chart it shows you is a real chart computed from your real numbers, not an illustration of what a chart might look like.

You don't need to know how to code. You need to know what question you want answered about your data. If you can describe the analysis to a data analyst — "I want to see revenue by region, broken out by month, with last year's data for comparison" — you can describe it to Code Interpreter. The translation from your description to working Python is what it handles.

What Code Interpreter can do with your data:

Statistical analysis: Mean, median, variance, correlation, regression — described in plain English and executed on your actual file. "Which variables correlate most strongly with customer churn?" → Code Interpreter runs the correlation matrix and ranks the results.

Chart generation: Bar charts, line charts, scatter plots, heat maps — any chart you describe gets generated from your real data. "Show me monthly revenue by product line for the last 12 months as a stacked bar chart" → downloadable PNG of exactly that chart.

Data cleaning: "Find all rows where the date column is formatted inconsistently and standardize them" → Code Interpreter finds the inconsistencies, shows you what it found, fixes them, and gives you a clean file to download.

Automation of repetitive calculations: If you calculate the same metrics every month from a new data export, Code Interpreter can build the script once and reuse it every month with new data.

Your first Code Interpreter session — what to expect step by step:

Here's exactly what happens in a real Code Interpreter session, from the moment you upload a file to the moment you have a real analysis result.

Step 1: Enable Code Interpreter. In ChatGPT, click the paperclip icon or look for the Code Interpreter option in a new chat. Some plans enable it by default.

Step 2: Upload your file. Drag and drop or click to upload. ChatGPT accepts CSV, Excel, JSON, PDF, and many other formats. For data analysis, CSV and Excel are the most common.

Step 3: Describe your analysis in plain language. You don't need to say "write a Python script." Just say what you want: "Show me total revenue by region for each month. I want a bar chart comparing Q1 and Q2 side by side."

Step 4: ChatGPT runs the code and shows results. You see the Python code it wrote (you can read it or ignore it), the output of running that code, and any charts it generated. The charts are real. You can download them.

Step 5: Iterate on the analysis. "Can you add a trend line to that chart?" "What are the top 5 outliers in this dataset?" "Filter to just the rows where Region = 'West' and rerun the analysis." Each follow-up runs more code on your actual data.

Step 6: Download the outputs. Charts, cleaned data files, summary tables — anything Code Interpreter produces can be downloaded. Your analysis results are real files, not screenshots.

The natural progression from chat to Code Interpreter:

Most people discover Code Interpreter after trying to describe their data in chat and realizing descriptions aren't the same as analysis. You paste five rows of sample data into chat and ask for a trend analysis. ChatGPT gives you a description of what trend analysis on that data would show, not actual trend analysis. That's when you realize: you need to upload the real file and get real results.

Chat is for thinking about data. Code Interpreter is for actually analyzing it. The transition happens when you have a real file and need a real answer, not a description of an answer.

Describing analysis to Code Interpreter — a template that works:
"I'm uploading [describe the file and what it contains]. I want to [describe the analysis]. The output should be [describe format: chart type, table format, key metrics]. Please also [any additional analysis or cleaning]."

Three things to specify in every Code Interpreter request:

  1. What the file contains: "This is monthly sales data by rep and product line for 2024." Context helps ChatGPT interpret ambiguous column names.
  2. The specific question: "Which reps had the highest revenue growth rate, not just the highest total revenue?" Precise questions produce precise answers.
  3. The output format: "Show me a bar chart sorted by growth rate, highest to lowest, with the exact percentages labeled." Specific format requests produce output you can actually use.

Code Interpreter is also the bridge to building reusable analysis tools. Once ChatGPT has written Python that analyzes your monthly data correctly, you can ask it to save that analysis as a script. Next month, run the same script on new data. The analysis runs in seconds instead of the hour it currently takes to build the charts manually.

An end-to-end example — monthly sales analysis in one session:

You upload your January sales data. You ask: "Show me total revenue by region, rank the top 10 sales reps by revenue, and flag any rep who is more than 20% below their Q4 2023 average." Code Interpreter runs the analysis, produces a sorted bar chart and a flagged table. You ask: "Save this as a reusable script I can run on February's data when I upload it next month." It saves the Python. Next month, you upload the February file and run the same script. Two months later, you have a reliable monthly reporting workflow that took one setup session and runs in minutes.

This is what Code Interpreter is for: taking the analysis that lives in your head and making it run automatically on real data every time.

Code Interpreter runs actual Python on your uploaded data files. You don't need to code — you need to describe your analysis clearly. The six-step session walkthrough shows you exactly what to expect. The three things to specify in every request — what the file contains, the specific question, and the output format — give you the template. The path: ask about data in Chat to clarify your questions, then switch to Code Interpreter to actually run the analysis. Next: how to choose the right mode for any task using a simple three-question framework.

exercises.prompt: You upload a sales CSV to Code Interpreter. What's the best way to ask for a revenue comparison by region?
options: This file has monthly sales by rep, product, and region for 2024. Create a bar chart showing total revenue by region, sorted highest to lowest, with revenue amounts labeled on each bar.
Write Python code to calculate revenue by region
exercises.explanation: Option B describes what the file contains, specifies the analysis (revenue by region), the chart type (bar chart), the sort order (highest to lowest), and the labels (revenue amounts). That's everything Code Interpreter needs to produce exactly what you want.
hints: Code Interpreter needs to know: what's in the file, what analysis to run, and what the output should look like.

exercises.prompt: Walk through the journey from manual data processing to a reusable analysis script in Code Interpreter.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "From Manual to Automated Analysis", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You produce a monthly sales report from a CSV export. It takes you 3 hours in Excel every month. Let's move this to Code Interpreter."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "You have your January CSV. What do you tell Code Interpreter first?", "question": "What's the most efficient first message?", "options": ["Analyze this CSV", "This is monthly sales data with columns for Rep, Region, Product, Revenue, and Month. Show me: (1) total revenue by region, (2) top 10 reps by revenue, (3) any rep more than 20% below their prior month. Produce a bar chart for revenue by region.", "What's in this file?", "Write code to process CSV files"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Describing the file content and the three specific outputs in one message is efficient. Code Interpreter runs all three analyses and produces the chart in one step."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Code Interpreter produced the analysis and chart. You can download them. Now you realize you'll do this every month. What do you ask?", "question": "What's the next step to make this repeatable?", "options": ["Great, I'll redo this manually next month", "Save the Python code you just wrote as a reusable script. I want to upload February's CSV next month and run the same analysis.", "Delete the file and start over", "Ask for a different analysis"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Asking Code Interpreter to save the script makes the analysis reusable. Next month you upload the new file and run the saved script — same analysis in minutes instead of 3 hours."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "The script is saved. Next month, you upload February's CSV and run the script. The analysis runs in 30 seconds. Now you want to add a new metric: month-over-month revenue growth rate by region.", "question": "How do you add the new metric without rebuilding the whole script?", "options": ["Start a new Code Interpreter session from scratch", "Tell Code Interpreter: Add a month-over-month growth rate calculation by region to the existing script. Show it as a line chart overlaid on the bar chart.", "Calculate it manually in Excel", "Remove the bar chart and replace it with the growth rate"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "You can layer enhancements onto the existing script over time. Each enhancement makes the analysis more complete without rebuilding what's already working."}]}
exercises.explanation: The path from manual to automated: describe the full analysis clearly, ask Code Interpreter to save it as a reusable script, then layer enhancements over time. You can enhance the script without starting over.
hints: Think about what makes each step a natural evolution, not a jump.

lessons.title: Choosing the Right Mode
lessons.description: A decision framework for picking Chat, Canvas, or Code Interpreter for any task. Practical scenarios you'll actually face.

sections.content:
Three questions tell you which mode to use — and they work for every task, every time:

1. Do I have a data file to analyze? Yes → Code Interpreter. No → continue to question 2.

2. Am I editing a long document or code file with targeted changes? Yes → Canvas. No → continue to question 3.

3. Is this a thinking, writing, or brainstorming task? Yes → Chat. Nearly everything starts here and expands when friction tells you to.

That's the entire framework. Three questions, clear answers every time. The beauty is that each question rules out two modes at once, so you rarely need all three to make the decision. If you answer "yes, data file" to question 1, you're done — it's Code Interpreter. If you answer "yes, long document editing" to question 2, it's Canvas.

Why the framework matters: The cost of using the wrong mode isn't just inefficiency — it's frustration that makes ChatGPT feel less useful than it actually is. When Code Interpreter isn't the right tool, you might conclude that ChatGPT can't do real data analysis. It can — you just weren't in the right mode. The three questions eliminate that mismatch.

Real-scenario decision tree — 10 situations, which mode, why:

Situation 1: You need to decide whether to hire a full-time employee or a contractor. Data file? No. Long document editing? No. Thinking task? Yes. → Chat.

Situation 2: Your CFO sent you a 60-page financial report. You need to find every assumption underlying the revenue forecast. Data file? No (it's a PDF to read, not analyze). Long document editing? No. → Chat (paste the PDF content or paste key sections).

Situation 3: You have a 500-row CSV of customer transactions and want to know which product categories drive the most revenue. Data file? Yes. → Code Interpreter.

Situation 4: You need to write a difficult message to a team member about their performance. No files, just a sensitive writing task. Data file? No. Long editing? No. → Chat.

Situation 5: You have a 2,500-word proposal draft. The executive summary needs to be rewritten without touching the rest. Data file? No. Long document with targeted edit? Yes. → Canvas.

Situation 6: You have monthly survey data as a CSV from 500 respondents. You want to see which answers correlate with customer satisfaction scores. Data file? Yes. → Code Interpreter.

Situation 7: You're preparing for a salary negotiation and want to think through your arguments. Data file? No. Thinking task? Yes. → Chat.

Situation 8: You have a 200-line Python script that needs error handling added to three specific functions. Long code file with targeted edits? Yes. → Canvas.

Situation 9: You want to write three versions of the same announcement — one for employees, one for customers, one for investors. Thinking and writing? Yes. → Chat.

Situation 10: You have weekly data exports from your CRM. You want a chart comparing this month's pipeline to last month's. Data file? Yes. → Code Interpreter.

Common traps and how to avoid them:

Describing data in Chat when you should use Code Interpreter. The symptom: you paste five rows of sample data and ask for analysis. ChatGPT gives you a description of what the analysis would show, not actual analysis on your real data. The fix: upload the real file to Code Interpreter. You'll know it's time when you want a real answer, not a plausible-sounding description of an answer.

Regenerating full documents for small edits. The risk: you ask Chat to "fix paragraph 4" and get back a completely new 2,000-word document. You lose the parts that were working. The fix: open Canvas for any document long enough that regenerating it fully wastes time you spent on earlier sections.

Using Canvas for short, iterative generation. Canvas shines for long documents that need surgical editing. For short outputs — a 150-word email, a single paragraph — Chat's regeneration speed is fine. Don't add Canvas overhead for tasks where a quick regeneration is genuinely faster.

Staying in one mode for everything because it's familiar. Each mode has a specific sweet spot. Chat for thinking and writing, Canvas for long-form editing, Code Interpreter for real data. Recognize the friction signals (describing data, losing document sections, wanting targeted edits) and switch modes when the task calls for it.

Start simple, expand as friction appears. The three-mode framework doesn't mean you need to use all three modes — it means you have them available when the task calls for them.

Most people begin with Chat and that's exactly right. Chat is where you get comfortable with ChatGPT's capabilities, develop your prompting instincts, and discover what kinds of help it's genuinely useful for. When you hit regeneration waste consistently — you'll know it when you feel it — that's when you try Canvas. When you find yourself describing data instead of analyzing it, that's when you try Code Interpreter.

The progression is driven by your own friction, not by a predetermined schedule. Don't force the migration to other modes before the friction appears. Just be aware that the friction is a signal, and the right mode is the answer to it.

The compound effect over 90 days: Most people who follow this progression — Chat in week 1, Canvas by week 3, Code Interpreter by week 6 — report that by day 90 they've reclaimed several hours per week from tasks that ChatGPT now handles faster. Each mode saves time in a specific domain. The savings compound because each improvement frees you to do more higher-leverage work — which you use Chat to think through better. The whole system gets better together.

Three questions: Data file? Long document editing? Thinking or writing? Chat for thinking and writing with no files. Canvas for targeted edits on long documents or code. Code Interpreter for real computation on actual data files. The ten-scenario decision tree gives you practice applying the framework to real situations. Start with Chat, expand when friction tells you to. Avoid using descriptions as a substitute for real data analysis. You now have the complete mental model for using ChatGPT at every level — and a 90-day progression to compound the value.

exercises.prompt: Match each scenario to the right ChatGPT mode using the three-question framework.
exercises.correct_answer: {"Deciding whether to hire a contractor or a full-time employee":"Chat — thinking task, no files, no long editing","Analyzing an uploaded spreadsheet of customer survey results":"Code Interpreter — data file with real analysis needed","Editing section 2 of a 3,000-word strategic report without touching other sections":"Canvas — long document with targeted edit","Drafting a difficult Slack message to your team":"Chat — thinking/writing, no files","Running Python to clean and standardize a messy CSV of 800 rows":"Code Interpreter — real data file, actual computation","Building a long proposal iteratively, section by section":"Canvas — long-form document with incremental edits"}
exercises.explanation: The three questions sort every task: Data file? (Code Interpreter) Long editing? (Canvas) Thinking or writing? (Chat). Run the questions and the answer falls out.
hints: For each scenario, ask: data file? long document editing? thinking or writing?

exercises.prompt: Three people are stuck using the wrong ChatGPT mode. Diagnose the mismatch and recommend the right one.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Mode Mismatch Clinic", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "Three real situations where someone is using the wrong ChatGPT mode. Your job: spot the mismatch and recommend the fix."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Sarah pastes five rows of sample sales data into Chat every week and asks for trend analysis. ChatGPT describes what a trend analysis would show, but it's never based on her real numbers.", "question": "What's the mismatch?", "options": ["She should stop using ChatGPT entirely", "She should switch to Code Interpreter — upload the real CSV and get real computed analysis", "She should switch to Canvas to create a chart", "She should paste more rows"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Describing data gets descriptions. Code Interpreter runs actual Python on her real file and produces actual computed results and charts. The switch is just uploading the file instead of pasting rows."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Marcus has a 2,000-word proposal in ChatGPT Chat. Every time he asks to improve one paragraph, ChatGPT regenerates the entire document and he loses the sections he spent 30 minutes refining.", "question": "What's the mismatch?", "options": ["He should use Chat with more precise prompts", "He should switch to Canvas — it makes targeted edits to specific sections without regenerating everything", "He should write shorter proposals", "He should accept the regenerated version"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Regeneration waste on long documents is the classic Chat-to-Canvas signal. Canvas edits surgically. He points at a paragraph; it edits that paragraph."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Priya is using Canvas to write short social media captions (under 100 words each). She opens Canvas, gets a caption, asks for a revision, and finds the Canvas overhead slower than just using Chat.", "question": "What's the mismatch?", "options": ["She should switch to Code Interpreter for social content", "She should go back to Chat — Canvas overhead is for long documents, not short iterative generation", "She should stop writing captions", "She should use a longer format"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Canvas overhead is justified for long documents where targeted edits save time. For 100-word captions, Chat's fast regeneration is simply better. Tools serve tasks — don't add complexity where it doesn't save time."}]}
exercises.explanation: Mode mismatches waste time. Chat-to-Code Interpreter: describing data instead of analyzing it. Chat-to-Canvas: losing sections on long document edits. Canvas-to-Chat: adding overhead for short iterative content. Spot the friction, switch the mode.
hints: Look for the friction or waste in each person's current approach.

exercises.prompt: Think about your own work. Describe one task you do regularly and explain which ChatGPT mode you'd use for it and why. Apply the three-question framework.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"Think about a real task from your own work — something you do at least weekly. Describe the task and explain which ChatGPT mode (Chat, Canvas, or Code Interpreter) you'd use. Apply the three-question framework: (1) Do I have a data file to analyze? (2) Am I editing a long document with targeted changes? (3) Is this a thinking, writing, or brainstorming task?","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["I","my","we","our","every","weekly","daily","monthly","regularly"],"label":"Described a real personal task","points":2},{"keyword_group":["chat","canvas","code interpreter","ChatGPT"],"label":"Named a specific ChatGPT mode","points":2},{"keyword_group":["file","data","spreadsheet","csv","long document","edit","thinking","writing","brainstorm","analysis"],"label":"Connected to the framework criteria","points":3},{"keyword_group":["because","since","so","which means","that's why","the reason"],"label":"Explained the reasoning","points":2}],"max_points":9,"pass_threshold":5,"ideal_prompt":"Every Monday I compile a weekly business review from last week's sales data. I have a CSV export from our CRM. I upload it to Code Interpreter and ask for: total revenue by product, top 10 deals closed, and any rep who missed their weekly target. The framework: data file = yes (Code Interpreter), long document editing = no, thinking task = no. Code Interpreter runs real analysis in 30 seconds instead of the 2 hours I used to spend in Excel.","tips":["Pick a task you actually do, not a hypothetical.","Walk through all three questions explicitly.","If you're unsure between two modes, explain why — that shows real understanding."]}
exercises.explanation: Applying the framework to your own work makes it stick. The three questions (data file? long editing? thinking or writing?) sort any task into the right mode. The best learning happens when you map it to something real.
hints: Pick something you actually do, not a hypothetical. Walk through the three questions.
